text: This is a video illustration demonstrating bladder sparing robotic pelvic excentration in a T-phorectal cancer involving the prostate and its feasibility to reduce short-term complications. A thirty-four-year-old gentleman presented with altered bowel habits and on evaluation was found to have a growth at three centimeters from the anal verge. A biopsy was suggestive of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. MRI of the pelvis showed a lower rectal cancer involving the prehypergastric fascia, the denonvillous fascia, and the prostate. He was placed on neoadjuvant chemoradiation and on its immediate completion, went into acute bowel obstruction. An emergency diversion sigmoid stoma was performed, following which four cycles of Kapox was given.

The surgical team positions the small bowel, considering the previous sigmoid stoma, and proceeds with a medial to lateral resection. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and artery (IMA) are delineated. The nodes at the root of the IMA are cleared, and the IMA is ligated and divided after the origin of the left colic artery, followed by the ligation of the IMV.

The surgeon performs lateral peritoneal dissection, mobilizing the colon up to the splenic flexure. A linear stapler is fired distal to the existing sigmoid stoma. During posterior pelvic dissection, the prehypogastric fascia is excised due to involvement. Anterior pelvic dissection is initiated, identifying the vas deferens on both sides before ligation.

The surgeon temporarily anchors the bladder to the abdominal wall for improved visualization during anterior pelvic dissection. The denonvillous fascia and seminal vesicles are resected en bloc with the tumor.

The surgeon releases the temporary anchorage of the bladder and distends it with saline. The anterior resection continues by entering the red sear space. The bladder neck is identified, and the urethra is transected at its neck, with the transection level highlighted on the sagittal MRI section. The bladder neck is then closed.

The surgeon performs a suprapubic cystostomy, permanently anchoring the bladder to the anterior abdominal wall. The procedure then moves to the perineal aspect, where an extralevator abdominoperineal resection (APR) is conducted, transecting the urethra at the bulbar component. A VY advancement flap is created, and the final specimen displays the en bloc resection of the seminal vesicles and prostate.

The immediate complications that could occur from a urinary conduit or a neobladder were avoided in this patient while obtaining an R-zero resection and preserving the short-term oncological outcomes.
